meta.title: 9.2 Consensus Algorithms
meta.description: Raft and Paxos. How distributed systems agree on values. Leader election, log replication, and safety guarantees.

blocks.prompt: Five servers need to agree on who is the leader. Two servers cannot communicate with the other three (network partition). How do the remaining three safely elect a new leader without risking two leaders?

blocks.content:
## The consensus problem

Multiple nodes must agree on a value (e.g., the next entry in a replicated log) even if some nodes fail or are unreachable. Requirements: (1) **Agreement:** All non-faulty nodes agree on the same value. (2) **Validity:** The agreed value was proposed by some node. (3) **Termination:** Non-faulty nodes eventually decide.

## Raft: consensus made understandable

Raft separates consensus into three sub-problems:

### 1. Leader election
- Nodes are in one of three states: follower, candidate, leader
- Followers become candidates if they do not hear from the leader (election timeout)
- Candidate requests votes from all nodes. Wins if it gets majority.
- Only one leader per term (terms are monotonically increasing integers)

### 2. Log replication
- Leader receives client requests, appends to its log
- Replicates log entries to followers
- Entry is committed when majority acknowledges
- Leader notifies followers of committed entries

### 3. Safety
- A candidate cannot win an election if its log is behind other nodes
- Committed entries are never lost (guaranteed by majority overlap)

**Why majority matters:** With 5 nodes, any two majorities (3 nodes each) share at least one member. This member has the committed entry, ensuring it is not lost.

## Raft vs Paxos

| | Raft | Paxos |
|---|---|---|
| Understandability | Designed to be understandable | Notoriously difficult |
| Leader | Strong leader model | Multi-decree; leader is optimization |
| Used by | etcd, Consul, CockroachDB | Google Chubby, Spanner |
| Implementation | Many open-source implementations | Fewer; often custom variants |

**For interviews:** Understand Raft deeply. Mention Paxos as the theoretical predecessor. Know that both guarantee safety (never disagree) and liveness (eventually decide, if majority is available).

blocks.question: A 5-node Raft cluster loses 2 nodes. Can it still process requests? What about 3 nodes lost?
blocks.answer: **2 nodes lost: Yes.** The remaining 3 nodes form a majority (3/5). The leader (or a newly elected one) can still commit entries because majority agreement is achievable. **3 nodes lost: No.** The remaining 2 nodes cannot form a majority (need 3/5). The cluster cannot elect a leader or commit new entries. It is unavailable until at least one more node recovers. This is the CP tradeoff — the cluster sacrifices availability to prevent inconsistency (split brain). **Design implication:** Use 3 nodes (tolerates 1 failure) or 5 nodes (tolerates 2 failures). Even numbers are bad — 4 nodes still only tolerates 1 failure (need 3/4 majority) with more overhead.

blocks.content: Raft provides consensus through leader election, log replication, and majority-based commits. A cluster of N nodes tolerates (N-1)/2 failures. Use odd numbers of nodes (3 or 5). Raft is the preferred choice for new systems; Paxos is the theoretical foundation.

blocks.prompt: Consensus tells nodes what happened. Module 9.3 covers distributed clocks — how nodes agree on WHEN things happened.
